claims.claim: The finite tie-aware Best-of-N law predicts selected real utility for a fixed score/utility pool.
Controlled contact and multimodal toys exhibit low-energy tail miscalibration: selected energy decreases while real utility falls.
A small learned contrastive EBM can over-optimize an observation-limited low-energy tail, and calibrated/value-shaped scoring improves high-N utility.
A PyTorch MLP IBC-style EBM trains with decreasing contrastive loss and produces finite exact-law selected-utility curves, showing the aligned-tail regime alongside the failure regime.
The repository produces a guarded Meta-World reach-v3 benchmark artifact with finite reward, energy, compute, and exact-law diagnostics.
A tail-aligned EBM can represent two valid modes better than explicit MSE regression, while a shortcut energy can still fail at high N.
More EBM inference compute trades energy evaluations and latency proxy against real utility, and added candidates can over-optimize energy.
Pilot-label calibration, value shaping, and support penalties repair selected-tail utility in controlled toy settings.
Pilot-label count and support-penalty sweeps quantify how much repair signal is needed to recover high-N selected-tail utility.
Optional benchmark adapters are guarded and do not support core claims unless dependencies and runs are present.
Real robot validation is future work, not a supported repository claim.
Forbidden clone and overclaim statements are treated as disallowed, not supported findings.

not_clone_audit.check: raw EBM compared to calibrated/value-shaped EBM
no real-robot validation claim in supported claims
does not imply EBMs always work
does not imply Best-of-N always helps
does not duplicate WAM/JEPA/diffusion failure wording as the EBM claim
